content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CorvIMIssIoN
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916)322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
July 30, 1998
Jane Moore
Director, Communication Services and Program Development
Riverside County Office of Education
3939 Thirteenth Street
Post Office Box 868
Riverside, California 92502-0868
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-171
Dear Ms. Moore:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Riverside County
Office of Education regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
Under the facts below, may the Riverside County Office of Education (“RCOE”) include
photographs of the Riverside County Superintendent of Schools and RCOE board members in its
annual report?
CONCLUSION
Yes. Pursuant to Section 89001 and Regulation 18901 you may not send over 200 copies
of the annual report in a single calendar month, since it features an elected officer affiliated with
the RCOE, and the costs of distribution are paid for with public funds. However, an annual
report sent from the RCOE to another governmental agency or officer does not count toward the
200 mark. Also, an annual report sent to employees, officers, deputies and other staff of the
RCOE does not count toward the 200 mark. Finally, items picked up and unsolicited requests for
the annual report do not count toward the 200 mark. Considering the above, it does not appear
that the RCOE will be sending over 200 copies of the annual report in a single calendar month
that do not fall within an exception.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-171
Page 2
FACTS
You are the Director of Communication Services and Program Development for the
Riverside County Office of Education. You would like to include photographs of your county
superintendent of schools and your county board members in the RCOE annual report.
Some reports are distributed through your JET Mail Service on its regular daily delivery
to school districts throughout the county, including 27 copies to school district superintendents,
27 to 139 copies to the presidents and members of school district boards of education, and 7
copies to the RCOE board.
Other reports are distributed internally, including 120 copies to the RCOE Leadership
Team Members, RCOE employees, and 15 copies to the PTA council presidents and officers
housed in your office. The remainder are distributed via U.S. Mail to various others, including
57 copies to other county superintendents in California, 5 copies to college and university
presidents, 5 copies to college and university departments of education, 11 copies to members of
the school district organization committee, 2 copies to the California Department of Education, 2
copies to legislative advocates, 35 copies to chamber of commerce presidents and education
chairs, 2 copies to legal firms who serve as RCOE legal counsel, 35-50 copies to others, such as
people who serve on committees for RCOE projects.
The total number of reports printed would be no more than 500. After distribution, the
remaining copies would be used in folders for individuals who tour your office or for individuals
who request information about the role of the county office.
ANALYSIS
Mass Mailings
Section 89001 states:
“No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public
expense.”
Regulation 18901 (copy enclosed) interprets Section 89001:
“(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b), a mailing is
prohibited by section 89001 if all of the following criteria are met:
(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at
his or her residence, place of employment or business, or post
office box. For purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a


File No. A-98-171
Page 3
videotape, record, or button, or a written document.
(2) The item sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, or
(B) ]ncludes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to
an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer.
(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public
moneys; or
(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00
are paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or
printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as
permitted by this regulation.
(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in
a single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b).
(b) [provides exceptions to the general rule].”
RCOE’s Annual Report
The annual report (1) will be sent to a recipient at his or her residence or place of
employment or business, (2) will feature an elected officer
2 affiliated with the agency that
prepared the mailing, namely the superintendent and the RCOE board members, and (3) will be
paid for with public funds. In addition, more than two hundred unsolicited reports are planned to
be sent.
An annual report left for pickup in a public location or distributed at a public meeting
does not qualifr as an item sent. (Regulation 18901(a)(l); Lavagetto Advice Letter, No. A-90-
199.) Therefore, the copies picked up by individuals who tour your office are not items sent
within the meaning of Regulation 18901(a)(l), and do not count toward the 201 mark. However,
a newsletter or announcement that is disseminated to third parties with the intention that the third
parties will further distribute the newsletter or announcement counts toward the 201 mark of
Regulation 18901(a)(4). (Leidigh Advice Letter, No. A-95-350.)
An “unsolicited request” is defined in Regulation 18901 (c)(4). An item mailed to
individuals who request information about the role ofthe county constitutes an unsolicited
request for a single response. Therefore, a single mailing to a person who specifically requests
information about the RCOE does not count toward the 201 mark either.
2 “Features an elected officer” is defmed in Regulation 1 890 1(c)(2).


File No. A-98-171
Page 4
Even after considering unsolicited requests and the requirement that the item be sent,
subsections (1)-(4) of Regulation 18901 are met in this case. Therefore, the proposed mailing
would result in a violation of Section 89001, unless an exception or exceptions under subdivision
(b) of Regulation 18901 are applicable.
Subsections (b)(3) and (b)(4) ofRegulation 18901 provide relevant exceptions to the
mass mailing prohibition:
“(3) Any item sent in the normal course ofbusiness from one
governmental entity or officer to another governmental entity or
officer.
(4) Any intra-agency communication sent in the normal course of
business to employees, officers, deputies, and other staff.”
It appears that a large majority of the reports that you plan to send out at public expense
will go to another governmental entity or officer or to the RCOE’s employees, officers, deputies,
and other staff during the normal course ofbusiness.
3 Those reports planned to be sent to
another governmental entity or officer during the normal course ofbusiness include the reports
being sent to college and university presidents, college and university departments of education,
members of the school district organization committee, and the California Department of
Education. Those reports planned to be sent to employees, officers, deputies and other staff
during the normal course ofbusiness include those sent to school district superintendents,
presidents and board members of school district boards of education, your legislative advocates,
your legal firm, the Riverside County Board of Education, and the Riverside County Office of
Education Leadership Team Members.
4
That leaves 15 copies to the PTA officers, 35 copies to the chamber of commerce
presidents and education chairs, and 35-50 copies to others as needed, such as people who serve
on committees for RCOE projects -- for a total of 100 copies that do not fall within an exception
and count toward the 201 mark. Since no more than 200 copies will be sent out in a single
calendar month that do not fall within an exception, the RCOE may distribute the annual report
as planned.
Please note that staff is relying on your characterization of the recipients of the mailing detailed during a
telephone conversation on July 23, 1998. The Commission is not the fmder of fact when providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
‘ Presumably, the legislative advocates and members of the legal firm are public officials, i.e., are
employees or consultants. (Section 82048.) However, even if the presumption is incorrect, the number of
qualifying mailings sent will not be greater than 200.


File No. A-98-171
Page 5
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Marte Castaflos
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

photographs of the Riverside County Superintendent of Schools and RCOE board members in its
annual report?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Pursuant to Section 89001 and Regulation 18901 you may not send over 200 copies
of the annual report in a single calendar month, since it features an elected officer affiliated with

200 mark. Also, an annual report sent to employees, officers, deputies and other staff of the

the annual report do not count toward the 200 mark. Considering the above, it does not appear
that the RCOE will be sending over 200 copies of the annual report in a single calendar month
